name: College Research & Writing
description: A half-credit course preparing students for the research and writing demands of college coursework. Students develop skills in formulating research questions, evaluating sources, constructing arguments, integrating evidence, and following academic citation standards through a culminating research project.

modules.title: Developing Research Questions
modules.objectives: Distinguish between a topic, a research question, and a thesis; transform a broad topic into a focused, arguable research question; evaluate research questions for feasibility and scholarly relevance.
modules.readingNote: Focus on how OpenStax Writing Guide describes moving from interest areas to researchable, arguable questions.
modules.videoNote: Watch the Khan Academy lesson on crafting focused research questions.
modules.video2Note: Review how to narrow a broad topic into a specific, manageable research question.
modules.practiceNote: Practice evaluating and improving research questions in context.
modules.assignment: Choose a topic you are passionate about and develop three candidate research questions. Select the strongest one and write a 300-word rationale explaining why it is focused, arguable, and researchable.

modules.title: Library & Database Research
modules.objectives: Use Boolean operators and advanced search filters in academic databases; identify peer-reviewed journal articles; navigate library catalog systems and interlibrary loan resources.
modules.readingNote: Read the OpenStax Writing Guide section on database searching strategies and managing sources efficiently.
modules.videoNote: Watch the tutorial on finding quality sources using academic databases and search strategies.
modules.video2Note: Review how to use Boolean operators (AND, OR, NOT) to refine database searches.
modules.practiceNote: Practice identifying the most relevant information from research sources.
modules.assignment: Using an academic database (JSTOR, Google Scholar, or ProQuest), conduct a search related to your research question from Module 1. Find five peer-reviewed articles. Record the search terms used and write a 200-word reflection on what made your search effective.

modules.title: Evaluating Sources Critically
modules.objectives: Apply the CRAAP test to evaluate sources; distinguish between primary, secondary, and tertiary sources; identify bias, credibility, and relevance in academic and popular sources.
modules.readingNote: Study the OpenStax section on source evaluation criteria including currency, relevance, authority, accuracy, and purpose (CRAAP).
modules.videoNote: Watch the Khan Academy lesson on distinguishing credible from unreliable sources.
modules.video2Note: Review how to evaluate websites and news sources for bias and accuracy using the SIFT method.
modules.practiceNote: Practice the source credibility evaluation exercises.
modules.assignment: Apply the CRAAP test to three of the five sources you found in Module 2. Write a 300-word evaluation table identifying each source's strengths and weaknesses and whether you will use it in your research paper.

modules.title: Note-Taking & Information Organization
modules.objectives: Distinguish effective note-taking strategies; avoid accidental plagiarism through accurate paraphrasing and quotation; use digital tools to organize research notes efficiently.
modules.readingNote: Read the OpenStax guidance on effective note-taking, paraphrasing strategies, and maintaining source attribution.
modules.videoNote: Watch the tutorial on organizing research notes and maintaining source integrity.
modules.video2Note: Review the Cornell Note-taking System and how it applies to research note organization.
modules.practiceNote: Practice paraphrasing passages without altering meaning.
modules.assignment: Create a research notes document for your research question. Record three direct quotes, three paraphrases, and three summaries from your sources. Include full source information for each note. Write a 150-word reflection on the difference between paraphrasing and summarizing.

modules.title: Annotated Bibliography
modules.objectives: Write a complete annotated bibliography entry in MLA or APA format; summarize, assess, and reflect on each source; explain how each source relates to the research argument.
modules.readingNote: Study the OpenStax guide to writing effective annotations that summarize, evaluate, and connect sources to your argument.
modules.videoNote: Watch the annotated bibliography walkthrough with sample entries in MLA and APA formats.
modules.video2Note: Review annotated bibliography examples and common formatting errors to avoid.
modules.practiceNote: Practice citation formatting exercises in MLA and APA styles.
modules.assignment: Write an annotated bibliography with five sources related to your research question. Each annotation should be 100–150 words and include: a summary of the source, an evaluation of its credibility, and an explanation of how you plan to use it in your paper.

modules.title: Thesis & Argument Development
modules.objectives: Construct a strong, arguable thesis statement; distinguish between weak and strong thesis claims; develop a logical argument structure using evidence and reasoning.
modules.readingNote: Read the OpenStax Writing Guide section on developing argumentative thesis statements and the difference between a topic sentence and a thesis claim.
modules.videoNote: Watch the thesis development tutorial covering arguability, specificity, and complexity.
modules.video2Note: Review how to revise a weak thesis into a strong, arguable claim using the Socratic method.
modules.practiceNote: Practice identifying and strengthening thesis statements in argument analysis exercises.
modules.assignment: Write three draft thesis statements for your research paper. Using feedback criteria from the module, self-evaluate each thesis and revise the strongest one into a final thesis. Submit all three drafts and the final thesis with a 200-word explanation of your revision choices.

modules.title: Outline & Paper Organization
modules.objectives: Create a detailed outline for a multi-page research paper; organize body paragraphs logically; use topic sentences to signal each paragraph's function.
modules.readingNote: Study the OpenStax section on outlining strategies including alphanumeric, decimal, and mind-map approaches for research papers.
modules.videoNote: Watch the step-by-step research paper outline construction tutorial.
modules.video2Note: Review how to use a reverse outline to check an existing draft's logic and structure.
modules.practiceNote: Practice reordering paragraphs and ideas into logical sequence.
modules.assignment: Create a full-sentence outline for your research paper. Your outline must include: an introduction section with thesis, at least four body sections with topic sentences and evidence points, and a conclusion section. The outline should be approximately one page.

modules.title: Writing Introductions & Conclusions
modules.objectives: Write an engaging research paper introduction with a hook, context, and thesis; craft a conclusion that synthesizes findings without mere repetition; avoid common introduction and conclusion errors.
modules.readingNote: Read the OpenStax guidance on effective introduction hooks (anecdote, statistic, question) and conclusion synthesis strategies.
modules.videoNote: Watch the tutorial on writing powerful academic introductions and conclusions.
modules.video2Note: Review common introduction and conclusion mistakes and how to avoid them in college writing.
modules.practiceNote: Practice evaluating and improving introduction and conclusion paragraphs.
modules.assignment: Write an introduction and conclusion for your research paper. The introduction must include a hook, background context, and your thesis statement. The conclusion must synthesize your argument and offer a broader implication. Each should be 150–200 words. Submit both with a 150-word self-reflection on your choices.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following best describes a strong research question?
options: A broad statement of fact that everyone already agrees with
A focused, arguable question that requires investigation and evidence
A question answered with a simple yes or no
A restatement of the paper's topic in question form
quizQuestions.answer: A focused, arguable question that requires investigation and evidence
quizQuestions.explanation: A strong research question is specific, debatable, and complex enough to require a sustained investigation through evidence.

quizQuestions.question: A thesis statement differs from a research question in that a thesis:
options: Identifies the topic without taking a position
Is always a single sentence without supporting details
Presents the writer's central argument or claim in response to the research question
Lists all the subtopics the paper will cover
quizQuestions.answer: Presents the writer's central argument or claim in response to the research question
quizQuestions.explanation: A thesis statement is the answer to the research question—it presents the writer's controlling claim that the paper will support.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Which of the following best describes a strong research question" is: A focused, arguable question that requires investigation and evidence.
options: True
False
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. A focused, arguable question that requires investigation and evidence is correct.

quizQuestions.question: Boolean operators in database searching include which of the following terms?
options: LIKE, SIMILAR, CLOSE
AND, OR, NOT
FIND, SEARCH, MATCH
WITH, NEAR, ABOUT
quizQuestions.answer: AND, OR, NOT
quizQuestions.explanation: Boolean operators AND, OR, and NOT allow researchers to combine, expand, or exclude search terms to control the scope of database results.

quizQuestions.question: A peer-reviewed journal article is most reliable for academic research because it:
options: Was published on a well-known website
Passed evaluation by expert scholars before publication
Is freely available to anyone online
Was written by a college student as a course assignment
quizQuestions.answer: Passed evaluation by expert scholars before publication
quizQuestions.explanation: Peer review means the article was critically evaluated by independent experts in the field before publication, ensuring scholarly quality.

quizQuestions.question: When searching a database, placing quotation marks around a phrase (e.g., "social media addiction") instructs the database to:
options: Search for either word separately
Exclude all articles that mention the phrase
Search for the exact phrase rather than individual words separately
Limit results to the most recent five years only
quizQuestions.answer: Search for the exact phrase rather than individual words separately
quizQuestions.explanation: Phrase searching with quotation marks retrieves results where words appear together as a unit, making searches more precise.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Boolean operators in database searching include which of the following terms" is: AND, OR, NOT.
options: True
False
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. AND, OR, NOT is correct.

quizQuestions.question: In the CRAAP test, the 'A' for Authority refers to evaluating:
options: The accuracy of facts in the source
The availability of the source online
The credentials and expertise of the author or publisher
The academic level of the language used
quizQuestions.answer: The credentials and expertise of the author or publisher
quizQuestions.explanation: Authority assesses whether the author has relevant expertise, credentials, or institutional affiliation that qualifies them to write on the topic.

quizQuestions.question: A primary source in research is best described as:
options: The most important source in your bibliography
An original document or firsthand account of an event or study
A textbook that synthesizes many sources on a topic
A Wikipedia article with extensive citations
quizQuestions.answer: An original document or firsthand account of an event or study
quizQuestions.explanation: Primary sources are original materials—research studies, diaries, speeches, interviews, and original data—not interpretations of those materials.

quizQuestions.question: The SIFT method for evaluating online sources instructs users to first:
options: Search immediately for more information on the topic
Save the article and use it without further verification
Stop and investigate the source before accepting its claims
Skim the article's conclusion to determine its reliability
quizQuestions.answer: Stop and investigate the source before accepting its claims
quizQuestions.explanation: The 'S' in SIFT stands for 'Stop'—resist the impulse to immediately share or accept information and first investigate the source's credibility.

quizQuestions.question: Accidental plagiarism most commonly occurs when a student:
options: Copies an entire article and submits it as their own work
Uses a source's ideas in their own words but fails to cite it
Writes original arguments without consulting any sources
Cites too many sources in a single paragraph
quizQuestions.answer: Uses a source's ideas in their own words but fails to cite it
quizQuestions.explanation: Accidental plagiarism typically results from paraphrasing or summarizing a source's ideas without providing proper attribution.

quizQuestions.question: Paraphrasing differs from summarizing in that paraphrasing:
options: Is always shorter than the original passage
Covers only the main idea of an entire work
Restates a specific passage in one's own words while maintaining its level of detail
Requires no citation because the words are the writer's own
quizQuestions.answer: Restates a specific passage in one's own words while maintaining its level of detail
quizQuestions.explanation: Paraphrasing rewrites a specific passage at approximately the same length but in new words, while summarizing condenses an entire work to its main idea.

quizQuestions.question: The Cornell Note-taking System is effective for research because it:
options: Requires copying all source text verbatim for accuracy
Organizes notes into cue, note, and summary sections for review and retrieval
Eliminates the need to identify page numbers for citations
Limits note-taking to bullet points only
quizQuestions.answer: Organizes notes into cue, note, and summary sections for review and retrieval
quizQuestions.explanation: The Cornell system structures notes into an area for cues/questions, main notes, and a summary, facilitating efficient review and connection-making.

quizQuestions.question: When taking research notes, which practice best prevents patchwork writing?
options: Copying exact phrases from sources and reformatting them later
Recording source ideas in your own words immediately and noting the source
Mixing quotations from multiple sources into a single note
Relying on memory instead of recording exact source information
quizQuestions.answer: Recording source ideas in your own words immediately and noting the source
quizQuestions.explanation: Transforming source language immediately into your own words while noting the source prevents unintentional copying and patchwork assembly later.

quizQuestions.question: An annotated bibliography entry typically includes all of the following EXCEPT:
options: A complete citation in the required format
A summary of the source's content
An evaluation of the source's credibility
A full reprint of the source's abstract
quizQuestions.answer: A full reprint of the source's abstract
quizQuestions.explanation: Annotated bibliography entries include citation, summary, evaluation, and reflection in the student's own words—not a reprint of the original abstract.

quizQuestions.question: The evaluative component of an annotated bibliography entry asks the writer to:
options: Copy the source's conclusions verbatim
Assess the source's credibility, bias, and usefulness for the research project
List all other sources cited by the author
Describe the source's formatting and layout
quizQuestions.answer: Assess the source's credibility, bias, and usefulness for the research project
quizQuestions.explanation: The evaluation section critically examines the source's authority, objectivity, methods, and relevance to the writer's research.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "An annotated bibliography entry typically includes all of the following EXCEPT:" is: A full reprint of the source's abstract.
options: True
False
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. A full reprint of the source's abstract is correct.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "The evaluative component of an annotated bibliography entry asks the writer to:" is: Copy the source's conclusions verbatim.
options: True
False
quizQuestions.answer: False
quizQuestions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Assess the source's credibility, bias, and usefulness for the research project.

quizQuestions.question: A strong thesis statement must be:
options: A question that the paper will attempt to answer
A statement of universally accepted fact
An arguable claim that the paper will support with evidence
A broad overview of the paper's topic
quizQuestions.answer: An arguable claim that the paper will support with evidence
quizQuestions.explanation: A thesis must be contestable—a claim that someone could disagree with—and the paper's purpose is to persuade the reader through evidence.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is an example of a weak thesis statement?
options: Although social media has positive uses, its unregulated design exploits teenage psychology and exacerbates mental health crises.
The opioid epidemic has had devastating effects on American families.
Schools should replace standardized testing with portfolio assessment because it more accurately measures diverse student abilities.
While free trade agreements boost GDP, they disproportionately harm low-wage domestic manufacturing workers.
quizQuestions.answer: The opioid epidemic has had devastating effects on American families.
quizQuestions.explanation: This statement is too obvious and vague—it makes no specific argument and could not be reasonably debated.

quizQuestions.question: The Toulmin model of argument consists of which core elements?
options: Introduction, body, and conclusion
Claim, data, and warrant
Thesis, evidence, and summary
Hook, context, and thesis
quizQuestions.answer: Claim, data, and warrant
quizQuestions.explanation: Stephen Toulmin's model of argument identifies the claim (what you assert), data (the evidence), and warrant (the reasoning connecting evidence to claim) as the core structure.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "A strong thesis statement must be:" is: An arguable claim that the paper will support with evidence.
options: True
False
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. An arguable claim that the paper will support with evidence is correct.

quizQuestions.question: A full-sentence outline differs from a topic outline in that a full-sentence outline:
options: Uses only Roman numerals for main sections
Expresses every entry as a complete sentence revealing the content of each section
Is used only for informal writing assignments
Lists topics without indicating how they relate to the thesis
quizQuestions.answer: Expresses every entry as a complete sentence revealing the content of each section
quizQuestions.explanation: Full-sentence outlines use complete sentences for every entry, forcing the writer to articulate the actual claim of each section rather than just a vague label.

quizQuestions.question: A topic sentence in a body paragraph performs which essential function?
options: Restates the thesis in identical wording
Provides the section's strongest piece of evidence
States the paragraph's controlling idea and its relationship to the thesis
Concludes the paragraph's argument and transitions to the next section
quizQuestions.answer: States the paragraph's controlling idea and its relationship to the thesis
quizQuestions.explanation: A topic sentence identifies what the paragraph is about and connects it to the paper's overall argument.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "A full-sentence outline differs from a topic outline in that a full-sentence outline:" is: Expresses every entry as a complete sentence revealing the content of each section.
options: True
False
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Expresses every entry as a complete sentence revealing the content of each section is correct.

quizQuestions.question: An effective introduction hook is designed to:
options: State the thesis immediately before any context is provided
Provide a complete summary of the paper's argument
Capture the reader's attention and create interest in the topic
List all the sources the paper will use
quizQuestions.answer: Capture the reader's attention and create interest in the topic
quizQuestions.explanation: An introduction hook—whether a striking statistic, anecdote, or provocative question—draws the reader in before establishing context and thesis.

quizQuestions.question: A conclusion in academic writing should avoid:
options: Restating the thesis in new words
Discussing broader implications of the argument
Simply repeating the introduction paragraph verbatim
Suggesting areas for further research
quizQuestions.answer: Simply repeating the introduction paragraph verbatim
quizQuestions.explanation: A conclusion should synthesize the argument and extend its implications—not mechanically repeat the introduction or body points.

quizQuestions.question: Background context in an introduction serves to:
options: Introduce the sources that will be cited in the body paragraphs
Give readers the necessary information to understand the thesis
Present the counterargument before the thesis is stated
List the paper's organizational structure explicitly
quizQuestions.answer: Give readers the necessary information to understand the thesis
quizQuestions.explanation: Context bridges the hook and thesis by providing key background information that frames why the research question matters.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "An effective introduction hook is designed to:" is: Capture the reader's attention and create interest in the topic.
options: True
False
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Capture the reader's attention and create interest in the topic is correct.

questions.question: A strong research question must be:
options: Answerable with a simple yes or no
A broad topic statement without a specific angle
Focused, arguable, and requiring evidence-based investigation
Already answered by existing consensus
questions.answer: Focused, arguable, and requiring evidence-based investigation
questions.explanation: A strong research question is specific, contestable, and complex enough to drive a sustained investigation.

questions.question: The Boolean operator AND in a database search:
options: Expands results to include either search term
Narrows results to include both search terms
Excludes all articles containing either term
Searches for exact phrase matching
questions.answer: Narrows results to include both search terms
questions.explanation: AND requires both search terms to appear in results, narrowing the search to relevant sources addressing both concepts.

questions.question: In the CRAAP test, the letter 'P' (Purpose) asks evaluators to consider:
options: Whether the source was published in a print format
Why the source was written and whether it reflects bias or a particular agenda
The page length of the source
Whether the author's purpose matches the research question topic
questions.answer: Why the source was written and whether it reflects bias or a particular agenda
questions.explanation: Purpose assesses the source's intent—to inform, persuade, sell, or entertain—and whether that intent introduces bias.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "To avoid plagiarism when paraphrasing, writers must:" is: Restate the idea in their own words and sentence structure AND cite the source.
options: True
False
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: True. Restate the idea in their own words and sentence structure AND cite the source is correct.

questions.question: An annotated bibliography entry's 'reflective' section requires the writer to explain:
options: How many times the source was cited in the paper
The source's publication history and previous editions
How the source will contribute to and connect with the research argument
The source's formatting and citation style
questions.answer: How the source will contribute to and connect with the research argument
questions.explanation: The reflection connects the source to the writer's specific argument, explaining its relevance and planned use.

questions.question: A strong thesis statement must be arguable, which means it:
options: Presents a fact that most scholars agree is accurate
Makes a claim that reasonable people could disagree with
Is supported by at least five scholarly sources
Is written in the first person with the phrase 'I believe'
questions.answer: Makes a claim that reasonable people could disagree with
questions.explanation: An arguable thesis invites debate and requires supporting evidence—it is not a fact but a position that needs to be demonstrated.

questions.question: A full-sentence outline is preferred over a topic outline for research paper planning because it:
options: Is shorter and easier to create before writing
Forces the writer to articulate the actual claim of each section
Allows writers to use bullet points for all entries
Requires only a list of sources without organizational structure
questions.answer: Forces the writer to articulate the actual claim of each section
questions.explanation: Full-sentence outlines reveal the actual argument of each section, helping writers identify logical gaps before drafting.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "An effective introduction hook's primary purpose is to:" is: Provide the complete argument of the paper upfront.
options: True
False
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Engage the reader's interest before building context and thesis.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the 'quote sandwich' technique?
options: Using two short quotations surrounding a longer paraphrase
Introducing evidence with a signal phrase, presenting it, then analyzing its significance
Placing all quotations in the introduction and conclusion
Using only block quotations for sources longer than four lines
questions.answer: Introducing evidence with a signal phrase, presenting it, then analyzing its significance
questions.explanation: The quote sandwich ensures evidence is properly framed and analyzed: introduce the source, present the quotation, explain its connection to the argument.

questions.question: In MLA format, the in-text citation (Smith 42) indicates:
options: Smith is the title of the article, published on page 42
The author's last name is Smith and the cited content is on page 42
The source was written in 1942 by an author named Smith
Smith is the 42nd source on the Works Cited page
questions.answer: The author's last name is Smith and the cited content is on page 42
questions.explanation: MLA in-text citations direct readers to the full source information on the Works Cited page using author last name and page number.

questions.question: Revision focuses on which level of a draft?
options: Correcting spelling and punctuation errors
Fixing comma splices and run-on sentences
Evaluating argument strength, organization, and evidence quality
Checking that the citation page is formatted correctly
questions.answer: Evaluating argument strength, organization, and evidence quality
questions.explanation: Revision addresses the content and structure of the argument—the 'global' elements—while proofreading handles surface-level errors.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Specific, actionable peer feedback is best exemplified by which comment" is: 'Your topic sentence in paragraph 3 does not connect to your thesis—try revising it to include the word "equity" to match your argument.'.
options: True
False
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: True. 'Your topic sentence in paragraph 3 does not connect to your thesis—try revising it to include the word "equity" to match your argument.' is correct.

questions.question: A primary source is best defined as:
options: The first source found in a database search
An original, firsthand document such as a research study, speech, or diary entry
A textbook chapter that synthesizes secondary sources
A Wikipedia article with at least 20 citations
questions.answer: An original, firsthand document such as a research study, speech, or diary entry
questions.explanation: Primary sources are original materials—raw data, firsthand accounts—that secondary sources interpret and analyze.

questions.question: Patchwork writing is problematic because it:
options: Uses too many different citation styles in one paper
Stitches together source passages with minimal original analysis, failing to demonstrate the writer's thinking
Includes too many contrasting viewpoints without a clear thesis
Changes the argument midway through the paper
questions.answer: Stitches together source passages with minimal original analysis, failing to demonstrate the writer's thinking
questions.explanation: Patchwork writing lacks the writer's analytical voice and is a form of academic dishonesty even when sources are cited.

questions.question: The Toulmin model of argument identifies the 'warrant' as:
options: The main claim of the argument
The evidence used to support the claim
The logical reasoning that connects evidence to the claim
The counterargument that the writer must refute
questions.answer: The logical reasoning that connects evidence to the claim
questions.explanation: The warrant explains why the evidence supports the claim—the reasoning or assumption that makes the evidence relevant.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Which of the following research questions is the strongest example of an arguable, focused question" is: What is the history of social media?.
options: True
False
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Should social media platforms be legally liable for algorithmic amplification of extremist content?.

questions.question: In a database search, using the OR operator between two terms (e.g., teenagers OR adolescents):
options: Requires both terms to appear in every result
Excludes results that use either term
Broadens the search by retrieving articles containing either term
Searches for the exact phrase 'teenagers OR adolescents'
questions.answer: Broadens the search by retrieving articles containing either term
questions.explanation: OR expands search results by including all articles that contain at least one of the specified terms, useful for capturing synonymous concepts.

questions.question: A peer-reviewed source is preferable in academic research because:
options: Peer-reviewed articles are always freely available online
Expert reviewers have evaluated the research methodology and conclusions before publication
Peer-reviewed sources are shorter and easier to read than textbooks
Peer-reviewed articles never require citation
questions.answer: Expert reviewers have evaluated the research methodology and conclusions before publication
questions.explanation: Peer review is a quality control process in which independent experts assess the validity and rigor of research before it is published.

questions.question: Confirmation bias in the research process leads writers to:
options: Cite too many sources from the same publisher
Seek evidence that supports existing beliefs while discounting contradictory evidence
Overuse primary sources at the expense of secondary analysis
Forget to update sources when new editions are published
questions.answer: Seek evidence that supports existing beliefs while discounting contradictory evidence
questions.explanation: Confirmation bias compromises research integrity by causing writers to selectively gather and interpret evidence in ways that confirm their preexisting position.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "An annotated bibliography serves the research writing process by:" is: Helping the writer critically engage with sources before drafting the paper.
options: True
False
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: True. Helping the writer critically engage with sources before drafting the paper is correct.

questions.question: Which revision strategy helps writers identify logical gaps between their outline and their actual draft?
options: Reading the draft aloud to check for awkward phrasing
Creating a reverse outline of the completed draft
Checking each paragraph's formatting against the style guide
Counting the number of citations in each body paragraph
questions.answer: Creating a reverse outline of the completed draft
questions.explanation: A reverse outline extracts the actual argument of each paragraph, revealing where the draft diverges from the intended logical structure.

questions.question: A conclusion paragraph in a research paper should do all of the following EXCEPT:
options: Restate the thesis in new words
Synthesize the paper's main supporting points
Discuss the broader implications of the argument
Introduce new evidence and claims not covered in the body
questions.answer: Introduce new evidence and claims not covered in the body
questions.explanation: Conclusions synthesize what has already been argued—they do not introduce new evidence or arguments that the body paragraphs did not support.

questions.question: APA 7th edition in-text citations for a direct quotation require which three elements?
options: Author first name, publication title, and year
Author last name, year, and page number
Author last name, database name, and URL
Title, journal volume, and issue number
questions.answer: Author last name, year, and page number
questions.explanation: APA in-text citations for direct quotations require: (Author, year, p. page#) — for example, (Smith, 2021, p. 42).

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "The 'show, don't tell' principle in personal essay writing asks writers to:" is: Prove their GPA and test scores using specific numbers.
options: True
False
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Use narrative scenes and sensory details rather than abstract character claims.

questions.question: Transition sentences between body paragraphs serve which primary function?
options: Introducing new evidence that was not in the outline
Connecting the end of one paragraph's idea to the beginning of the next
Summarizing the entire paper's argument after each body section
Replacing the need for topic sentences in body paragraphs
questions.answer: Connecting the end of one paragraph's idea to the beginning of the next
questions.explanation: Transitions signal logical movement between paragraphs, guiding readers through the argument's progression.

questions.question: Descriptive peer feedback is preferable to evaluative feedback because it:
options: Allows the reviewer to assign a numerical score to the paper
Describes what the reviewer observed without rendering a judgment, giving the writer agency to decide
Evaluates whether the paper meets the assignment requirements
Compares the paper's quality to the best paper in the class
questions.answer: Describes what the reviewer observed without rendering a judgment, giving the writer agency to decide
questions.explanation: Descriptive feedback presents observations that the writer can act on, rather than judgments that can feel dismissive or unhelpful.

questions.question: When presenting research orally, slides should contain:
options: The full text of the paper formatted into bullet points
Minimal text with visuals and key terms that support the speaker's explanation
Every quotation cited in the written paper
Only the title and thesis, with no visual content
questions.answer: Minimal text with visuals and key terms that support the speaker's explanation
questions.explanation: Effective presentation slides serve as a visual aid—not a script—using key terms, data visualizations, and images to complement the speaker.

questions.question: The Common Application personal statement requires applicants to write no more than:
questions.answer: 650 words
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: 650 words.

questions.question: Integrating a counterargument into an academic research paper primarily serves to:
options: Prove that the writer agrees with all perspectives on the issue
Weaken the thesis by presenting opposing views
Demonstrate intellectual rigor and strengthen the argument by addressing objections
Fill required word count without adding new evidence
questions.answer: Demonstrate intellectual rigor and strengthen the argument by addressing objections
questions.explanation: Engaging counterarguments shows that the writer has considered multiple perspectives and can defend the thesis against the strongest objections.

questions.question: The SIFT method for evaluating online information stands for:
options: Search, Identify, Find, Track
Stop, Investigate, Find better coverage, Trace claims
Summarize, Interpret, Find sources, Test reliability
Scan, Index, Flag bias, Trust ranking
questions.answer: Stop, Investigate, Find better coverage, Trace claims
questions.explanation: SIFT is a lateral reading strategy: Stop before sharing, Investigate the source, Find better coverage from multiple outlets, and Trace claims to their original context.

questions.question: Which citation element distinguishes an MLA Works Cited entry from an APA References entry for a journal article?
options: MLA lists the author's last name while APA lists the first name only
APA places the year of publication directly after the author's name, while MLA places it near the end
MLA requires a DOI for all online articles while APA does not
APA uses quotation marks around article titles while MLA does not
questions.answer: APA places the year of publication directly after the author's name, while MLA places it near the end
questions.explanation: A key structural difference is that APA citation leads with the author name then year, while MLA places the year toward the end of the citation after journal volume/issue information.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "A revision memo demonstrates which writing competency" is: Ability to proofread for surface-level grammar errors.
options: True
False
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Metacognitive awareness—the ability to reflect on and evaluate one's own writing process and choices.

questions.question: Which of the following best exemplifies a dropped quotation error?
options: 'As Smith argues, "social media harms teenagers" (12).'
'Research shows that teens are affected by social media (Smith 12).'
'"Social media harms teenagers." This is an important concern.'
'According to Smith (2021), social media is linked to increased depression (p. 12).'
questions.answer: '"Social media harms teenagers." This is an important concern.'
questions.explanation: A dropped quotation appears without a signal phrase attributing it to a source and without analytical follow-up connecting it to the argument.

questions.question: Body paragraphs in a research paper should follow which organizational pattern?
options: Hook, context, thesis
Abstract, methodology, results, discussion
Topic sentence, evidence (with citation), analysis, transition
Introduction, counterargument, refutation, conclusion
questions.answer: Topic sentence, evidence (with citation), analysis, transition
questions.explanation: Effective body paragraphs open with a topic sentence, present cited evidence, analyze its significance for the argument, and transition to the next paragraph.

questions.question: College admissions officers most value personal statements that:
options: List all academic achievements and test scores in narrative form
Use complex vocabulary to demonstrate intelligence and preparation
Reveal authentic personality, specific experiences, and genuine self-reflection
Prove that the applicant has read many books in their intended major
questions.answer: Reveal authentic personality, specific experiences, and genuine self-reflection
questions.explanation: Admissions readers seek essays that offer insight into who the applicant genuinely is—their voice, values, and capacity for meaningful self-examination.
